name: NSQ Distributed Real-Time Messaging Platform
what_it_does: Open-source distributed real-time messaging platform with HTTP and TCP API — enabling high-throughput, fault-tolerant message passing between services with at-least-once delivery, no single point of failure, consumer-driven flow control, and an administrative HTTP API, built in Go and originally developed at Bitly for large-scale data pipeline processing.
use_cases: Building fault-tolerant message pipelines via NSQ for high-throughput data processing
Implementing pub/sub event distribution via NSQ for decoupled microservice communication
Processing at-least-once delivery queues via NSQ for reliable job processing
Building data pipelines for analytics ingestion via NSQ at Bitly/similar scale
Self-hosting a lightweight message queue via NSQ without Kafka complexity
not_for: Strict message ordering requirements (NSQ has no ordering guarantees per-topic)
Message persistence and replay (NSQ messages are in-memory, no disk replay like Kafka)
Teams needing managed cloud service (NSQ is self-hosted only)
best_when: A Go-native team needs a simple, operationally lightweight distributed messaging system — where NSQ provides horizontal scaling, fault tolerance, and at-least-once delivery without Kafka's operational complexity.
avoid_when: You need strict message ordering, message replay/replay, or a managed cloud messaging service.

auth.notes: No built-in authentication by default. Optional TLS for transport encryption. Optional authorization via auth-http-address (custom HTTP endpoint returns user permissions). No API keys. No OAuth2. Self-hosted only — deploy on your own infrastructure. Authorization delegated to external HTTP server via nsqd auth-http-address option.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Open source (MIT license) — completely free self-hosted
pricing.paid_tiers: No paid tiers; infrastructure costs only
pricing.notes: Created at Bitly (2012). MIT license. Written in Go. GitHub: nsqio/nsq (25,000+ stars). Three components: nsqd (daemon), nsqlookupd (topology), nsqadmin (web UI). At-least-once delivery (messages can be re-delivered). No message ordering guarantee. In-memory with optional disk overflow. Multi-subscriber (topic → channels → consumers). Consumer pull model (not push). Discovery via nsqlookupd. Used by: Bitly, Hailo, Path, Simplereach. Go client (go-nsq) is the reference implementation. Pynsq (Python), node-nsq (Node.js), nsq-j (Java). No Zookeeper dependency (unlike older Kafka). Simple HTTP admin API.

performance.rate_limits: Self-hosted — no limits beyond hardware capacity

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: HTTP API (nsqd publish at POST /pub; nsqadmin REST). TCP protocol for consumers. MIT open source. Go-native. At-least-once delivery. No auth by default. Self-hosted. Consumer pull model. Good documentation for Go users.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Message publication is fire-and-forget (at-least-once). Consumer side: messages may be re-delivered — implement idempotent consumers. No deduplication built-in. Use message IDs for application-level deduplication.
known_agent_gotchas: No authentication by default — must configure auth-http-address for access control
At-least-once delivery — consumers MUST be idempotent (messages can be re-delivered)
No message ordering guarantee — messages may arrive out of order
In-memory by default — messages lost if nsqd crashes without disk overflow configured
Three components required: nsqd + nsqlookupd + nsqadmin — all must be deployed
Consumer pull model via TCP protocol — not HTTP subscribe; HTTP only for publishing

security_score_components.security_notes: No built-in auth — must configure auth-http-address for authorization. TLS optional for transport. MIT open source — auditable Go code. User-controlled data residency. Authorization delegated to external HTTP server. Self-hosted means network perimeter is primary security. Admin API exposed on separate port — restrict access.
